title: Moisture Mapping Guide for Water Damage Inspections — Reading Placement & Insurance Documentation
description: Learn how to build a defensible moisture map for water damage insurance claims in Australia — reading placement strategy, grid documentation, and IICRC S500 compliance. RestoreAssist includes a built-in moisture mapping canvas with IICRC threshold overlays.
author: Phill McGurk | RestoreAssist
transcript:
## What Is a Moisture Map?

A moisture map is a scaled floor plan — or a simplified room diagram — that records moisture readings at specific, documented locations throughout the affected area. The map serves two purposes: it guides the drying strategy during the job, and it provides defensible insurance documentation that shows the full extent of damage at the time of inspection.

Under IICRC S500:2025, moisture mapping is a required component of both the initial assessment and the daily monitoring phase. The map must be updated each visit with new readings at the same grid points so that drying progression (or lack thereof) can be tracked and documented.

## Selecting Reading Locations

Reading placement should follow a systematic grid, not a random or intuitive approach. The standard approach is:

- **Floor readings**: One reading per 9 m² of affected floor area, placed at a consistent height (typically the geometric centre of each grid square)
- **Wall readings**: Readings at 0 cm (base), 30 cm, 60 cm, and 100 cm heights to document wicking extent
- **Control readings**: At least two readings from unaffected materials of the same type in the same building — used as the pre-loss reference for the drying goal
- **Ceiling/subfloor readings**: Penetrating probe or tramex-style readings where accessible

Each reading location should be assigned a unique identifier (e.g., W1-0, W1-30, W1-60 for wall 1 at 0, 30, and 60 cm height) that is consistent across all visits.

## Instrument Selection

The choice of moisture meter affects both accuracy and the type of reading recorded:

- **Pin-type meters** measure electrical resistance between two probes inserted into the material. Accurate for timber; less reliable for concrete or plasterboard.
- **Pinless (radio frequency) meters** measure moisture content in a defined depth profile without penetration. Useful for screening but affected by salts, density variation, and surface contamination.
- **Relative humidity probes** (in-wall or in-slab probes) provide the most accurate reading of moisture within enclosed cavities or concrete slabs.

For insurance documentation, all instrument readings should include the make, model, and calibration date of the instrument used.

## Documenting Affected vs Dry Materials

The moisture map should clearly differentiate between:

- Materials at or above the wet threshold for their material type (per IICRC S500:2025 Appendix C)
- Materials in the transitional zone (wicking or migration front)
- Unaffected materials confirmed as dry

Colour coding is standard practice: red or amber for wet/transition materials, green for confirmed dry. This visual distinction is essential when the map is submitted with an insurance claim, as it immediately communicates scope to the adjuster without requiring them to interpret raw numbers.

## Updating the Map Through the Job

The moisture map must be updated on each monitoring visit — typically daily for active drying jobs. The updated map should retain the original readings so that the full drying trend is visible. A final map confirming all readings at or below dry standard is required before issuing the drying completion certificate.
videoScript:
G'day, welcome to RestoreAssist. Today I want to walk you through how to build a moisture map that actually stands up to scrutiny from an insurance adjuster.

A moisture map isn't just a sketch with some numbers on it. Done right, it's a chronological record of the water damage extent, the drying progression, and the final confirmation that the structure is dry. It's one of the most important documents you produce on a water damage job.

Let's start with reading placement. You need a systematic grid, not guesswork. For floors, one reading per 9 square metres of affected area is the baseline. For walls, take readings at four heights — at the base, 30 centimetres, 60 centimetres, and 1 metre — and do this for every affected wall. That documents your wicking height, which determines your damage class.

And always — always — take control readings from unaffected materials of the same type somewhere in the building. You need that pre-loss reference as your drying goal baseline.

Every reading location gets a unique label that stays consistent across every visit. Wall 1 at 30 centimetres is W1-30 on day one, day two, day three, and the final confirmation. That consistency is what lets you show the drying trend.

On instrument choice: pin-type meters are your workhorse for timber. Pinless meters are good for quick screening but can be thrown off by salt contamination or density variation — don't rely on them as your only data source. For concrete slabs or enclosed cavities, you want a relative humidity probe for an accurate reading.

When you're presenting the map for insurance, colour code it. Red or amber for wet and transitional zones, green for confirmed dry materials. An adjuster can read a colour-coded map in 30 seconds. A page of raw numbers takes much longer and is easier to dispute.

Update the map on every daily visit, and keep all the historical readings visible — you want the full drying trend on record, not just the final day.

In RestoreAssist, the moisture mapping canvas lets you drop readings onto a floor plan, automatically colour-codes them against IICRC S500 material thresholds, and generates a dated moisture report ready for your insurance claim.

Head to restoreassist.app to see it in action.
